ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for grilled chicken steak with Mandi rice. FOODON_00002764 (chicken dish) used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for grilled chicken component; rice component not separately coded at composite level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for grilled chicken steak with Mandi spiced rice as a single dish. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) applied as the dominant protein component anchor using closest_parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite dish. SNOMED CT coverage of mixed restaurant dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of approximately 550 g, comprising a grilled chicken steak portion (estimated 200–250 g) alongside a generous serving of Mandi spiced basmati rice (estimated 300–350 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 171477) and Rice, white, long-grain, cooked, enriched (FDC ID 169756); composite estimate weighted ~45% chicken, ~55% rice by mass
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for Chicken Steak with Mandi Rice as a composite dish. Values are derived by weighted combination of USDA FDC entries for cooked chicken breast (FDC ID 171477) and cooked long-grain white rice (FDC ID 169756), with minor adjustments for olive oil, spices, and vegetables consistent with Mandi preparation. The input record's per_100g values (energy 136 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 5.45 g, carbohydrate 11.82 g) are broadly consistent with this weighted composite and have been retained with cross-validation against USDA component data. Sodium 218 mg/100g is plausible given spice blends and seasoning. Singapore HPB database does not contain a Mandi rice dish entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; basmati rice (white, boiled) GI ~58 used as primary analogous anchor; composite dish GI estimated at 56 accounting for protein and fat co-ingestion attenuating glycaemic response.
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Steak with Mandi Rice as a composite dish. Basmati rice (white, boiled) has a published GI of approximately 58 (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN). The composite dish GI is estimated at 56, reflecting a modest downward adjustment due to the protein content (10 g/100g) and fat content (5.45 g/100g) of the dish, which slow gastric emptying and attenuate postprandial glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 64.99) / 100 ≈ 36.4, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 11.82 × 5.5 = 65.01 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 218 mg/100g; a full 550 g plate delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, approaching 50–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, avoid adding soy sauce or salty condiments, and limit portion size to half a plate to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~56) and a high glycemic load per full serving (~36), driven by the basmati rice component; protein and fat co-ingestion partially attenuate postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the rice portion to approximately 150–200 g and prioritise the grilled chicken component to reduce total glycaemic load per meal to below 20.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.45 g/100g with zero trans fat; olive oil used in preparation provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids; cholesterol is moderate at 32.73 mg/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet when portion size is controlled; individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake from the full plate and avoid adding high-fat sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (cumin, coriander, cardamom) and olive oil MUFAs; nightshade vegetables (tomato, bell pepper) present which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should monitor symptom response after consuming this dish and request preparation without tomato or bell pepper if needed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Steak with Mandi Rice is a grilled chicken dish served alongside fragrant Mandi-spiced basmati rice, representing a Turkish and Middle Eastern culinary tradition adapted for the Singapore food service market. The chicken is typically marinated with a blend of Middle Eastern spices including cumin, coriander, cardamom, turmeric, and black pepper, then grilled over high heat to produce a charred, juicy exterior with a tender interior. The Mandi rice is cooked with aromatic spices and sometimes finished with a smoking technique using charcoal or wood chips, imparting a distinctive smoky fragrance. The dish is commonly accompanied by grilled tomatoes, bell peppers, and onions, with olive oil used as the primary cooking fat. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 136 kcal, 10 g protein, 5.45 g fat (of which 1.45 g is saturated), and 11.82 g carbohydrate. Sodium is moderately elevated at 218 mg/100g due to spice blends and seasoning. The dish is halal-certified and gluten-free. Clinically, the moderate glycemic index (~56) and high glycemic load per full plate (~36) require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content warrants attention for hypertensive individuals consuming a full 550 g plate. The low saturated fat and absence of trans fat make it broadly suitable for cardiovascular health when portion size is managed.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Chicken Steak with Mandi Rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted USDA FoodData Central composite of cooked chicken breast and cooked long-grain white rice, cross-validated against the input record's per_100g values which are internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate based on published basmati rice GI values from Atkinson et al. (2021). Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct dish-level measurement in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values retained from input record (provenance: ai_generated, grabfood) and cross-validated against USDA FDC weighted composite (cooked chicken breast FDC 171477 ~45% + cooked long-grain white rice FDC 169756 ~55%). Values are broadly consistent and have been accepted at medium confidence. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9) are estimated from USDA FDC fatty acid profiles for the component foods; these are approximate. 3. potassium_mg (220.0) is an estimate consistent with the input record's health_suitability narrative and USDA component data; treated as approximate. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Mandi dish preparation are not available as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; individual component data exist but reliable composite weighting for all micronutrients is not defensible without a dedicated dish analysis. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FDC data for grilled chicken and plain cooked rice; no hydrogenated fats used in preparation. 6. GI estimated at 56 based on analogous basmati rice GI of ~58 (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN) with downward adjustment for protein and fat co-ingestion effect. 7. The dish is described as Turkish/Middle Eastern in origin but is sold in Singapore via GrabFood; multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per G6 rules for Singapore-served dishes. 8. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_00002764 (chicken dish) used as closest_parent; FoodEx2 A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) used as closest_parent anchor for dominant protein component. foodex2_match_type corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to conform to allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. No SNOMED CT code identified.
